| Cilantro | |

| Category | Herbs and spices |

Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients

**Cilantro** is the fresh leafy part of the coriander plant. Particularly outside the USA, cilantro may go by other names including *dhania*, *coriander leaves*, *Chinese parsley*, or *Mexican parsley*.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Much like parsley, cilantro takes the form of bright green, feathery leaf bunches on long thin stalks.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) It is very aromatic, pungent, and somewhat citrussy,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) although a subset of the population has a genetic variation that makes them unpleasantly sensitive to cilantro's flavor.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Cilantro does not dry well, so it is best to purchase it fresh.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Look for bunches that are bright green and unwilted, with no slimy parts. It is best stored in the refrigerator or in a glass of water for up to a few days,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) after which it will start to wilt and degrade.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) To freeze cilantro, either blanch and drain the leaves or store them in oil.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Make sure to wash cilantro before use to remove any residual grit.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Fresh cilantro is an important ingredient in some Asian and South American cuisines.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) Chopped cilantro is also used as a garnish on many cooked dishes, much in the way of parsley.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) Cilantro should never itself be cooked as heat quickly destroys its delicate flavor.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

Depending on the desired use, a couple options exist as substitutions for cilantro. If you're after the flavor, culantro has a very similar aromatic profile.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) If you're looking for a garnish, flat-leaf parsley looks very similar and can be used pretty much identically.
